content.text: What Data Governance Means for LLM Systems

Data governance in an LLM context is the set of policies, processes, and technical controls that determine what data can be used with the model, who can access it, how it flows through the system, where it is stored, and when it must be deleted. It bridges legal obligations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA) with engineering decisions about logging, caching, retention, and tenant isolation.
LLM systems create governance challenges that traditional software does not face:
models can memorise and later reproduce sensitive data from their training corpus.
multi-turn conversations aggregate user data across sessions in ways users may not anticipate.
prompts sent to cloud LLM providers leave the application's infrastructure, raising data residency and sub-processor concerns.
debugging and monitoring logs routinely capture full prompt/response pairs, which may contain PII.

attrs.alt: LLM data lifecycle: Collection (purpose binding, consent, minimisation, classification, provenance) → Processing (access control, PII redaction, encryption, audit log, lineage) → Storage (encryption at rest, tenant isolation, retention, backup, geographic bounds) → Sharing (DPAs, transfer controls, API auth, export log, anonymisation) → Deletion (TTL, right-to-erasure, crypto-shredding, deletion receipt, backup purge)

content.text: Every piece of data entering or leaving an LLM system should be classified before processing. Classification drives every downstream control: which model can see it, whether it can be logged, how long it is retained.

Purpose binding means data collected for one reason cannot be used for another without additional consent. In an LLM pipeline this appears as: a conversation collected to answer a user query cannot be used to fine-tune the model without explicit consent.

Lineage records which source data influenced each LLM output. This is essential for GDPR right-to-erasure: when a user requests deletion, you need to know which model calls were influenced by their data and whether any cached outputs must also be invalidated.

Every data store in the LLM pipeline — conversation logs, prompt/response pairs, RAG embeddings, cache entries — needs an explicit retention policy with automated TTL enforcement. Manual purges are unreliable at scale.

import time
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RetentionPolicy:
    store_name: str
    ttl_days: int        # 0 = delete at session end
    legal_hold: bool     # if True, TTL is suspended until hold is released
    legal_hold_reason: str = ""

RETENTION_POLICIES: dict[str, RetentionPolicy] = {
    "conversation_logs":  RetentionPolicy("conversation_logs", ttl_days=30,  legal_hold=False),
    "prompt_cache":       RetentionPolicy("prompt_cache",      ttl_days=1,   legal_hold=False),
    "rag_embeddings":     RetentionPolicy("rag_embeddings",    ttl_days=365, legal_hold=False),
    "audit_logs":         RetentionPolicy("audit_logs",        ttl_days=365, legal_hold=False),
    "abuse_reports":      RetentionPolicy("abuse_reports",     ttl_days=730, legal_hold=True,
                                          legal_hold_reason="potential litigation"),
}

@dataclass
class StoredRecord:
    record_id: str
    store_name: str
    owner_id: str
    created_at: float   # Unix timestamp
    content_encrypted: bytes

def is_expired(record: StoredRecord) -> bool:
    policy = RETENTION_POLICIES.get(record.store_name)
    if policy is None or policy.legal_hold:
        return False
    if policy.ttl_days == 0:
        return True   # session-only; expired immediately
    age_days = (time.time() - record.created_at) / 86400
    return age_days > policy.ttl_days

def purge_expired(records: list[StoredRecord]) -> tuple[list[StoredRecord], int]:
    """Return (kept_records, purged_count)."""
    kept = [r for r in records if not is_expired(r)]
    purged = len(records) - len(kept)
    return kept, purged

When your application calls a cloud LLM provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.), the prompt leaves your infrastructure. This raises three governance questions: whether restricted data is allowed to leave, which provider agreements cover the data, and whether the provider uses inputs to train their models.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LLMProviderPolicy:
    provider: str
    data_classes_allowed: list[DataClass]
    data_processing_agreement: bool   # DPA in place
    trains_on_inputs: bool            # does provider use inputs for training?
    data_residency_regions: list[str] # e.g. ["us", "eu"]
    zero_retention: bool              # provider deletes inputs immediately

PROVIDER_POLICIES: dict[str, LLMProviderPolicy] = {
    "anthropic": LLMProviderPolicy(
        provider="anthropic",
        data_classes_allowed=[DataClass.PUBLIC, DataClass.INTERNAL, DataClass.CONFIDENTIAL],
        data_processing_agreement=True,
        trains_on_inputs=False,   # API inputs not used for training by default
        data_residency_regions=["us"],
        zero_retention=False,     # short-term retention for safety monitoring
    ),
    "local_ollama": LLMProviderPolicy(
        provider="local_ollama",
        data_classes_allowed=[DataClass.PUBLIC, DataClass.INTERNAL,
                               DataClass.CONFIDENTIAL, DataClass.RESTRICTED],
        data_processing_agreement=False,  # no third party
        trains_on_inputs=False,
        data_residency_regions=["local"],
        zero_retention=True,
    ),
}

def provider_allows(record: DataRecord, provider: str) -> bool:
    """Return True if the provider's policy permits processing this data record."""
    policy = PROVIDER_POLICIES.get(provider)
    if policy is None:
        return False  # unknown provider — deny by default
    if record.data_class not in policy.data_classes_allowed:
        return False
    controls = get_controls(record)
    if not controls["third_party_llm_allowed"]:
        return False
    return True

def route_to_provider(record: DataRecord) -> str:
    """Pick the appropriate LLM provider based on data classification."""
    for provider in ["local_ollama", "anthropic"]:
        if provider_allows(record, provider):
            return provider
    raise ValueError(f"No provider allows data class {record.data_class!r}")

GDPR Article 17 grants users the right to have their data erased. For an LLM system, this means: deleting stored conversations, purging cached completions, invalidating RAG embeddings derived from their data, and issuing a deletion receipt. Crypto-shredding (deleting the encryption key) is the standard approach for large encrypted stores where record-by-record deletion is impractical.

import hashlib, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass

logger = logging.getLogger("data_governance")

@dataclass
class ErasureReceipt:
    request_id: str
    user_id: str
    requested_at: str
    completed_at: str
    records_deleted: int
    lineage_nodes_invalidated: int
    encryption_keys_rotated: list[str]
    stores_purged: list[str]

def execute_erasure(user_id: str, request_id: str) -> ErasureReceipt:
    """
    Execute a right-to-erasure request.
    Steps:
    1. Identify all data and lineage nodes for this user.
    2. Delete records from all stores.
    3. Invalidate or re-encrypt derived caches.
    4. Rotate per-user encryption key (crypto-shredding).
    5. Emit audit log + deletion receipt.
    """
    from datetime import datetime

    # 1. Find affected lineage nodes
    affected_nodes = erasure_impact(user_id)

    # 2. Delete from stores (placeholder — replace with real DB calls)
    records_deleted = 0
    stores_purged = []
    for store_name in RETENTION_POLICIES:
        # In production: DELETE FROM <store> WHERE owner_id = user_id
        count = _mock_delete(store_name, user_id)
        if count > 0:
            records_deleted += count
            stores_purged.append(store_name)

    # 3. Invalidate lineage nodes
    for node_id in affected_nodes:
        _lineage.pop(node_id, None)

    # 4. Rotate encryption key (crypto-shredding)
    rotated_keys = [f"key:{hashlib.sha256(user_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]}"]

    # 5. Log
    receipt = ErasureReceipt(
        request_id=request_id,
        user_id=user_id,
        requested_at=datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        completed_at=datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        records_deleted=records_deleted,
        lineage_nodes_invalidated=len(affected_nodes),
        encryption_keys_rotated=rotated_keys,
        stores_purged=stores_purged,
    )
    logger.info("erasure_complete", extra={"receipt": receipt.__dict__})
    return receipt

def _mock_delete(store_name: str, user_id: str) -> int:
    """Placeholder; returns simulated delete count."""
    return 0
Label every data record with class + category before it enters the pipeline
Verify consent covers the processing purpose before each operation
Consent store check at API gateway layer
Never log RESTRICTED data in plaintext; hash or omit PII fields
Attach TTL to every stored record; run automated purge job daily
Route RESTRICTED data only to on-premise or zero-retention providers
Track which user data influenced each LLM output
Implement GDPR Art. 17 end-to-end: stores + caches + lineage + key rotation
Immutable audit log for every data access and governance decision
Append-only log, 365-day retention, shipped to SIEM

Data governance for LLM systems spans the full data lifecycle: classification at ingestion, purpose binding and consent verification before processing, encryption and audit logging during storage, provider routing controls for third-party API calls, and automated TTL enforcement with right-to-erasure support at deletion time. Lineage tracking is the connective tissue that makes erasure tractable — without it, you cannot identify which outputs derived from a given user's data. Governance is not a one-time setup; it requires ongoing policy review as regulations evolve and as new data flows are introduced into the system.

Classify data before it enters the pipeline, not after. Classification that happens at storage time is already too late — the data may have been logged, cached, or sent to a third-party provider before governance controls could act on it.
